content.full_text:
Jan 13 12:37 PM '92

CALIFORNIA CHAMBER of COMMERCE

January 10, 1992

Mr. Ben Davidian
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Ben:

As you know, I am no longer employed in the Governor's Office and have accepted a position with the California Chamber of Commerce as their Senior Vice President for Legislative Affairs. As such, I will be registered as a lobbyist for the Chamber.

I am unsure, however, as to the application of the Milton Marks' Post-government Employment Restrictions Act, Section 87406 of the Government Code, and would like some specific clarification. The statute indicates that no employee of a state administrative agency shall represent any other person for a period of 12 months "if the appearance or communication is made for the purpose of influencing administrative action...." Moreover, the statute indicates that as an employee of the Governor's Office, the prohibition applies to any administrative agency subject to the direction and control of the Governor.

Specifically, may I lobby Department Directors, Agency Secretaries, or their employees or representatives, to take support or oppose positions on legislation pending before the Legislature for passage or before the Governor for signature? Additionally, may I communicate with these offices or employees to receive or provide technical information on legislation or any other regulatory decision?

Although it is clear that I can represent the Chamber before the Legislature, there may arise certain situations within the legislative environment that involve Administration officials which require some clarification. For example, if a legislator invites me to a meeting where Administration (Governor's office or Agencies) representatives are also present, may I attend? The Chamber may be opposed to a legislator's bill in which the Administration has an interest. Thus, the legislator may convene a meeting to try to reach a consensus. Does the legislator have to omit the Administration official if I attend?

Finally, I would like to know which agencies are considered "subject to the direction and control of the Governor." For example, does Government Code Section 87406 apply to my contacts with the State Water Resources Control Board, since the Board members are appointed for a term and cannot be removed from office by the Governor?

Mr. Ben Davidian  
January 10, 1992  
Page 2

As you may recall, your Chief Counsel had verbally advised that I could make contacts with any Director or Agency Secretary regarding pending legislative issues. I will, however, be refraining from any of this activity pending your response, so I would appreciate your immediate attention to these issues.

Thank you.

Most cordially,

Allan Zaremberg

AZ:sg
